region_of_origin: Levant (Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, Palestine); also widely used in Turkish and broader Middle Eastern cuisine

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No dedicated FoodOn term for labneh exists; closest parent is yogurt food product (FOODON_03302571). Labneh is a strained yogurt product and is a derivative of yogurt.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code for labneh; mapped to fermented milk products category A00LC as closest parent. Labneh is a strained fermented dairy product.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No dedicated SNOMED CT code for labneh; closest parent is Yogurt (substance) 226760005. Labneh is a concentrated strained yogurt.

serving.description: 1 serving as a dip or spread
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of labneh as a dip or spread is approximately 100 g, often accompanied by olive oil, za'atar, and flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: Labneh (Strained Yogurt) — USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy, NDB No. 01116 (Plain whole-milk yogurt) adapted with concentration factor for strained labneh; cross-referenced with peer-reviewed compositional data for labneh from Al-Kadamany et al. (2003) and Tamime & Robinson (1999)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Labneh is produced by straining whole-milk yogurt to remove approximately 50–60% of its whey. Nutrient values are derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 171284) with a concentration factor of approximately 1.8–2.0x applied to protein, fat, and minerals, consistent with published labneh compositional studies (Al-Kadamany et al., 2003, LWT Food Science and Technology; Tamime & Robinson, Yoghurt: Science and Technology, 1999). Energy calculated from macros using Atwater factors. Sodium reflects typical salting at 0.5–1% NaCl during preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of dairy products — yogurt and fermented milk products; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for labneh was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 36 is estimated from the closest analogous food: full-fat plain yogurt (GI 36, glucose reference, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Labneh is a concentrated strained yogurt; its carbohydrate content is similar to or slightly lower than whole-milk yogurt due to partial whey removal. The GI is expected to be in the same low range. GL per 100 g serving = (36 × 11.5) / 100 = 4.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~390 mg per 100 g) from salting during preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume labneh in moderation and choose low-salt or unsalted varieties where available, as the sodium content from added salt during straining can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium intake targets of less than 2000 mg per day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~36) and moderate protein content supporting glycemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Labneh has a low glycemic index of approximately 36 and provides moderate protein (~7.5 g per 100 g), making it a suitable dairy option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in reasonable portions as part of a balanced meal; however, portion size should be monitored due to its moderate energy density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~6.8 g per 100 g) from full-fat dairy base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Labneh contains approximately 6.8 g of saturated fat per 100 g, which may contribute to LDL cholesterol elevation if consumed in large quantities; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion sizes and consider lower-fat yogurt-based alternatives, while benefiting from the calcium and potassium content that may support blood pressure regulation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Labneh (also spelled labaneh, labne, or labni; Arabic: لبنة) is a traditional Middle Eastern soft cheese made by straining plain yogurt — typically full-fat cow's milk yogurt — through a cheesecloth or fine strainer for 12 to 48 hours to remove the liquid whey. The result is a thick, creamy, spreadable product with a tangy, slightly sour flavour and a smooth, dense texture that is firmer than yogurt but softer than most hard cheeses. It is a staple of Levantine cuisine (Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, Palestine) and is also widely consumed across Turkey, Egypt, and the broader Arab world. In Singapore, labneh is served at Turkish and Lebanese restaurants as a mezze dip, typically drizzled with extra-virgin olive oil and dusted with za'atar or sumac, accompanied by warm flatbread or pita. Nutritionally, labneh is a concentrated dairy product providing approximately 166 kcal, 7.5 g protein, 10.5 g fat (of which 6.8 g is saturated), and 11.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. It is a good source of calcium (~260 mg per 100 g), potassium, riboflavin, and vitamin B12. Its glycemic index is low (approximately 36), making it suitable for glycemic management. The sodium content (~390 mg per 100 g) reflects typical salting during preparation and should be considered by individuals managing hypertension. Labneh is naturally gluten-free, vegetarian, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: Is labneh the same as yogurt?

confidence_reason: No exact labneh entry was found in a primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy whole-milk yogurt with a published concentration factor consistent with peer-reviewed labneh compositional studies. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (full-fat plain yogurt, GI 36) from the University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (whole-milk yogurt, FDC ID 171284) using a concentration factor of approximately 1.8–2.0x for protein, fat, calcium, and other nutrients, consistent with Al-Kadamany et al. (2003) LWT Food Science and Technology and Tamime & Robinson (1999). 2. Sodium (~390 mg per 100 g) reflects typical salting at approximately 0.5–0.8% NaCl; unsalted labneh would have substantially lower sodium. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: labneh is a dairy product containing no plant-derived fibre; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy yogurt data and consistent with all published labneh compositional analyses. 4. GI of 36 is an analogous estimate from full-fat plain yogurt (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for labneh was located in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy yogurt data with concentration factor applied; these are approximate and should be verified against a direct labneh analysis if available. 6. The food is listed under Turkish cuisine in the field survey source but labneh is a Levantine/Middle Eastern product; cuisine field is corrected to Middle Eastern with region_of_origin noting Levant. 7. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided as transliterations since labneh is not a Singaporean dish; the G6 mandatory multilingual rule applies to Singaporean dishes only. These names are provided as supplementary aliases for retrieval purposes.

improvement_actions_taken: Removed menu-style numbering '12.' from food_name to produce canonical 'Labneh'.
Corrected cuisine from 'Turkish' to 'Middle Eastern' with detailed region_of_origin noting Levant origin and Turkish usage.
Derived nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy yogurt with documented concentration factor per peer-reviewed labneh compositional studies.
Set dietary_fibre_g to 0.0 with confirmed-zero justification in notes (dairy product, no plant fibre).
Estimated GI of 36 from closest analogous food (full-fat plain yogurt) per Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care; documented estimation basis in gi_source.
Provided three ontology anchors all using allowed match_type values (closest_parent); gap notes explain absence of exact labneh codes.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance including Type 2 Diabetes with human_condition_name set correctly.
Built comprehensive aliases list with 18 entries and verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases array.
Documented all sentinel value usage and zero-value justifications in notes field.
Added multilingual transliterations for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi as supplementary aliases noting these are not mandatory for non-Singaporean cuisine.
